Paper Title: Sensor fusion of INS, odometer and GPS for robot localization
Method Category: Conventional wheel-INS-GPS fusion
Robot Platform: Differential-drive mobile robot model
Sensors: INS (accelerometer and gyroscope); wheel odometer; GPS
State Estimator: Six-state linear Kalman Filter followed by weighted fusion with GPS
Estimated State Variables: x- and y-acceleration; x- and y-velocity; x- and y-position
Propagation Model: Planar kinematic state propagation using acceleration and velocity
Measurement or Update Model: Accelerometer measurements and odometer-derived x-y position update the KF; the KF position output is then combined with GPS using a manually selected weight
Slip Handling Type: None
Contact Assumption: Conventional differential-drive wheels with implicit continuous rolling contact; no explicit contact model
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; GPS/KF weighting parameter alpha is selected manually for the operating scenario
Ground Truth Used: Simulated robot trajectory
Terrain or Environment: Arbitrary simulated planar trajectory; indoor/outdoor applicability is discussed but not experimentally tested
Main Finding: The weighted combination of KF and GPS output follows the simulated trajectory more closely and converges faster than the KF output alone
Quantitative Result: No numerical localization-error metric is reported; results are presented qualitatively in trajectory plots
Author-Stated Limitation: No explicit limitation of the complete method is stated; the paper notes GPS degradation indoors, gyroscope drift, and wheel-odometer errors caused by slip
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method does not detect or reject slip-affected wheel measurements and assumes a conventional continuous-wheel model
Relevance to This Thesis: Provides a basic example of conventional wheel-inertial fusion and shows why combining sensors can improve localization, but it does not address slip-aware fusion
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections V-VI; Section VII; Section VIII; Section IX
Notes: GPS is not included directly in the Kalman update; it is fused afterward using a weighted sum

Paper Title: A Comparison of Robust Kalman Filters for Improving Wheel-Inertial Odometry in Planetary Rovers
Method Category: Robust wheel-inertial odometry
Robot Platform: Pathfinder testbed planetary rover
Sensors: ADIS-16495 IMU (50 Hz); custom wheel encoder (10 Hz); NovAtel GPS/DGPS for initialization and reference truth
State Estimator: Error-state EKF (CoreNav) with Huber, covariance-scaling, and variational robust-filter variants
Estimated State Variables: 15-state error vector: attitude error; velocity error; position error; accelerometer bias; gyroscope bias
Propagation Model: IMU-based inertial error-state propagation
Measurement or Update Model: Wheel-odometry correction using longitudinal wheel speed and heading rate, with lateral/vertical nonholonomic pseudo-measurements and zero-velocity updates
Slip Handling Type: Robust de-weighting or covariance adaptation for erroneous wheel-odometry measurements; no explicit physical slip-ratio estimation
Contact Assumption: Conventional wheels with nonholonomic motion constraints and implicit continuous wheel-ground contact
Training or Prior Data Required: Filter parameters are manually tuned on one dataset and reused on similar datasets
Ground Truth Used: Post-processed differential GPS reference generated with RTKLIB
Validation Type: Real-rover field experiments on three datasets
Terrain or Environment: Low-feature rough Mars-analog terrain consisting of coal-ash residuals in Point Marion, Pennsylvania
Main Finding: Robust filtering generally improves the standard wheel-inertial EKF, but the best method depends on the dataset; variational filters perform best in two runs while Huber/covariance-scaling methods improve the third
Quantitative Result: RMS position error: CoreNav = 26.65/16.09/25.82 m for tests 1-3; best values were ORKF3 = 13.34 m (test 1), CSKF = 13.82 m (test 2), and ORKF2 = 11.67 m (test 3)
Author-Stated Limitation: Parameters can require retuning for different terrain; the small measurement vector limits inference of the measurement-noise distribution; future work calls for more datasets, longer distances, and online parameter learning
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method treats wheel odometry as an aggregate conventional-wheel measurement and does not model individual rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Directly relevant as an example of handling slip-corrupted wheel updates through robust innovation weighting rather than explicit slip compensation
Evidence Page or Section: Sections III and V-VII; Table 1; Tables 2-3; Conclusion and Future Work
Notes: CSKF uses a chi-square test with 4 degrees of freedom and threshold 9.488; an outlier in one component can de-weight the entire measurement vector

Paper Title: Multi-sensor Fusion SLAM Algorithm Considering Wheel Slip
Method Category: Slip detection and wheel-measurement rejection
Robot Platform: Dual-wheel differential-drive automated guided vehicle (AGV)
Sensors: WLR-720 LiDAR; ASM330LHH 6-axis IMU; drive-module wheel encoders
State Estimator: 15-state Error-State Kalman Filter for wheel-IMU odometry within a LiDAR SLAM system
Estimated State Variables: Position; velocity; attitude; accelerometer bias; gyroscope bias
Propagation Model: IMU integration propagates position, velocity, attitude, and IMU biases
Measurement or Update Model: Wheel-speed odometry corrects the IMU-propagated state during normal operation; LiDAR odometry and loop closure are used by the wider SLAM system
Slip Handling Type: Slip-ratio calculation, threshold-based slip detection, and rejection of wheel-odometry measurements
Contact Assumption: Fixed left/right wheel radii and conventional continuous differential-drive rolling contact
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; uses a predefined severe-slip threshold of slip ratio greater than 0.2
Ground Truth Used: Map and pose results are compared with the actual workshop environment; the exact independent pose-reference method is not clearly described
Terrain or Environment: Flat-floor robot-company workshop with walls, pillars, and machinery
Main Finding: Discarding wheel odometry during severe slip and relying on IMU data reduces slip-induced pose and mapping errors
Quantitative Result: Average pose error decreases from about 0.32 m to 0.07 m (about 78% reduction); reported map accuracy returns from about 85% to above 95%
Author-Stated Limitation: No explicit limitation of the complete method is stated; the paper discusses LiDAR sensitivity to environmental obstruction, IMU integration drift, and wheel-odometry error during slip
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The slip-ratio calculation assumes fixed-radius continuous wheels and therefore does not directly represent discontinuous rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Closely related because it rejects wheel measurements when slip is detected, but it uses a conventional slip-ratio model rather than PCV innovation consistency
Evidence Page or Section: Sections II-C and III; Section IV; Section V; Figures 13-14; Conclusion
Notes: The method section uses the term ESKF, while the conclusion refers more generally to EKF

Paper Title: Slip-Aware Motion Estimation for Off-Road Mobile Robots via Multi-Innovation Unscented Kalman Filter
Method Category: Model-based slip-aware wheel-IMU-GNSS fusion
Robot Platform: DUBHE four-wheeled skid-steered mobile robot
Sensors: GNSS; IMU; wheel encoders
State Estimator: Multi-Innovation Unscented Kalman Filter (MI-UKF)
Estimated State Variables: Planar position X-Y; heading; longitudinal velocity; lateral velocity; yaw rate; wheel-slip quantities derived from ICR estimates
Propagation Model: Planar kinematic process model driven by IMU acceleration measurements
Measurement or Update Model: GNSS and encoder-derived ICR position, orientation, and velocity measurements update the UKF; multiple historical innovations are stacked in the update
Slip Handling Type: Slip estimation and kinematic compensation through an asymmetric Instantaneous Center of Rotation model; wheel measurements are not rejected
Contact Assumption: Center of gravity at body center; flat ground; wheels on each side rotate at equal speed; all wheels remain in contact with the ground with equal constant radii
Training or Prior Data Required: No learned training model; requires kinematic assumptions and manually specified filter/noise parameters
Ground Truth Used: GNSS position is treated as the reference for position-error evaluation
Terrain or Environment: Flat dirt ground with straight, large-radius curved, and small-radius curved trajectories; tests include 4 m/s speed, about 100 m travel, and sideslip up to 40 degrees
Main Finding: The ICR slip model substantially improves odometry, and multi-innovation updates further improve position, orientation, and velocity estimates, particularly during turning
Quantitative Result: Position RMSE for En-DR/ICR/UKF+ICR/MI-UKF was 4.886/1.041/0.109/0.104 m, 14.695/5.850/0.701/0.677 m, and 0.224/0.139/0.017/0.014 m for the three scenarios; the authors summarize about 80% reduction from ICR modeling and about 10% additional reduction from MI-UKF
Author-Stated Limitation: Application to discontinuous state spaces needs further investigation, and the computational burden of MI-UKF may be significant
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: Its assumptions of constant-radius wheels, continuous contact, flat terrain, and equal wheel speeds on each side do not match independent discontinuous spoke contacts of a rimless wheel
Relevance to This Thesis: Useful contrast to the proposed method because it estimates and compensates slip through a vehicle kinematic model rather than rejecting inconsistent individual contact-velocity measurements
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Section III-A; Sections IV-V; Table 2; Section VI
Notes: GNSS is both fused by the estimator and used as the reference for reported position RMSE, so the evaluation reference is not fully independent

Paper Title: Sequential Sensor Fusion for Slip Estimation in Mobile Robots
Method Category: Proprioceptive slip estimation using track-to-track UKF fusion
Robot Platform: Simulated six-wheel lunar wheeled mobile robot with steerable wheels
Sensors: IMU; steering-angle measurements; wheel encoders
State Estimator: Network of local UKFs fused using Sequential SLR/UKF Track-to-Track Fusion (S2 UTF)
Estimated State Variables: Body longitudinal velocity; body lateral velocity; yaw rate; per-wheel-set longitudinal slip ratio derived from the estimated motion and encoder speed
Propagation Model: Continuous-time rigid-body velocity model driven by IMU linear and angular acceleration, discretized with Euler-Maruyama
Measurement or Update Model: Each wheel-set agent uses its steering angle as a measurement; local UKF tracks are fused sequentially while propagating cross-correlations
Slip Handling Type: Online longitudinal slip-ratio estimation; no measurement rejection
Contact Assumption: Ackermann steering geometry; negligible lateral slip angle; fixed-radius conventional wheels with continuous contact
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; model and covariance parameters are specified for the simulation
Ground Truth Used: True states from the high-fidelity software-in-the-loop digital-twin simulation
Validation Type: High-fidelity software-in-the-loop simulation with 200 Monte Carlo runs
Terrain or Environment: Simulated six-wheel lunar rover using a Vortex Studio wheel-soil contact model; high- and low-traction straight motion and circular motion
Main Finding: S2 UTF provides consistent real-time slip estimates while balancing accuracy and computational cost better than the compared consistent fusion methods
Quantitative Result: S2 UTF ANEES values were 1.1003, 1.0662, and 0.9288 for high-traction straight, low-traction straight, and circular tests; corresponding ARMSE values were 0.1983, 0.0012, and 0.0004
Author-Stated Limitation: The paper identifies integration of S2 UTF into the rover feedback-control system as immediate future work; no real-world experiment is presented
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The estimator assumes negligible lateral slip and continuous circular-wheel contact and is validated only in simulation, so it does not address uncertain rimless-wheel spoke contact
Relevance to This Thesis: Relevant as a purely proprioceptive planetary-rover slip estimator, but it estimates aggregate wheel-set slip rather than individual spoke-tip consistency
Evidence Page or Section: Sections IV-V; Equations 38-43; Table I; Section VI
Notes: The proposed method mainly targets fusion consistency and cross-correlation handling; all methods had the same order of accuracy in the reported tests

Paper Title: Classification-Based Wheel Slip Detection and Detector Fusion for Outdoor Mobile Robots
Method Category: Supervised learning-based immobilization detection and detector fusion
Robot Platform: DARPA LAGR autonomous mobile robot; four pneumatic tires; front-wheel differential drive
Sensors: Xsens MT9 IMU; front-wheel encoders or tachometers; differential GPS available but not used by the detector
State Estimator: Radial-basis-function support vector machine classifier; classifier output is also fused with a separate dynamic-model-based EKF detector
Estimated State Variables: No continuous vehicle state is estimated by the SVM; output classes are normal, immobilized, or optionally unknown
Propagation Model: None for the SVM classifier; the separate dynamic-model detector uses an EKF described in a companion work
Measurement or Update Model: Offline-trained SVM uses roll-rate variance, pitch-rate variance, high-frequency vertical-acceleration content, and optionally mean wheel angular acceleration; two fusion rules combine the SVM and model-based detector outputs
Slip Handling Type: Immobilization detection and detector fusion; no slip-ratio estimation or wheel-measurement correction
Contact Assumption: Conventional pneumatic wheels with implicit continuous wheel-ground contact
Training or Prior Data Required: Hand-labelled offline training data; 408 training instances collected over mud, loose mulch, and grass, including 14 immobilization events; SVM parameters selected by cross-validation
Ground Truth Used: Hand-labelled normal and immobilized intervals; absolute position information is not required
Validation Type: Real-robot outdoor experiments with offline processing
Terrain or Environment: Training on mud, loose mulch, and grasses; testing on a grassy hill and loose gravel mixed with dry soil
Main Finding: The SVM detects full immobilization using only proprioceptive signals, and fusing it with a model-based detector improves total detection accuracy while allowing a conservative zero-false-positive option
Quantitative Result: Four-feature SVM accuracy: 94.7%; IMU-only SVM accuracy: 92.0%; dynamic-model detector: 95.7%; Fusion 1: 91.7% with zero false positives; Fusion 2: 98.0% with zero false positives and six false negatives
Author-Stated Limitation: Future work is needed to assess SVM kernel choice, robot speed and configuration, alternate terrain types and situations, and fusion of more than two detectors
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method detects complete vehicle immobilization rather than partial or individual-wheel slip, requires hand-labelled offline training data, and does not model discontinuous rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Provides an early proprioceptive learning-based alternative to model-based slip detection, but its discrete whole-vehicle classifier differs from the proposed per-contact innovation-based rejection
Evidence Page or Section: Sections II-B, III-A–B, IV-C, and V; Table I
Notes: The uploaded paper is the 2007 ICRA conference paper. Do not cite it using a key that refers to the later 2009 journal version unless the bibliography entry is changed accordingly.

Paper Title: Estimating Wheel Slip of a Planetary Exploration Rover via Unsupervised Machine Learning
Method Category: Unsupervised learning-based slip classification
Robot Platform: ProtoInnovations Lunar All-Terrain Utility Vehicle (LATUV) planetary rover
Sensors: Four six-degree-of-freedom IMUs located near the wheels; wheel encoder or motor-position telemetry; drive and steering motor currents; RTK-GPS used only to construct ground-truth slip
State Estimator: Self-organizing map, k-means clustering, and autoencoder classifiers; no Kalman state estimator
Estimated State Variables: Discrete whole-rover slip class with two, three, four, five, or ten classes
Propagation Model: None; classifiers operate on filtered windows of proprioceptive sensor measurements
Measurement or Update Model: Sensor features from IMU, wheel velocity or acceleration, and motor current are mapped to unsupervised clusters or learned features and then associated with slip classes
Slip Handling Type: Slip classification or coarse slip estimation; no odometry correction, measurement rejection, or control compensation is implemented
Contact Assumption: Slip is defined using conventional wheel angular velocity and fixed radius; inputs from all wheels are combined, implicitly treating the rover-level slip state rather than individual contacts
Training or Prior Data Required: Two-fold cross-validation on collected field data; the learning is unsupervised, but labelled data is still required to associate clusters or features with physical slip classes and tune class boundaries
Ground Truth Used: Slip ratio computed by comparing RTK-GPS rover velocity with wheel-encoder angular velocity; a one-second median filter is applied
Validation Type: Real-rover field trials with repeated train/test splits
Terrain or Environment: Ten 15 m forward traverses up a sandy incline at fixed speeds from 0.05 to 0.25 m/s
Main Finding: Self-organizing maps consistently provide the highest and most repeatable classification accuracy, while k-means is fastest but least accurate and autoencoders provide intermediate performance
Quantitative Result: SOM achieves 93–97% accuracy for two-class immobilization detection, 87–93% for three classes, and up to 71% for ten classes; at 0.10 m/s, three-class accuracy is about 88%; k-means is 5–30 percentage points worse than SOM and autoencoders are 2–10 points worse
Author-Stated Limitation: The experiments cover only ten forward drives on one sandy slope; the methods combine all wheels and cannot identify which wheel is slipping; some supervision is required to assign clusters to slip classes; computational and storage requirements require further study
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method cannot distinguish individual slipping wheels or spokes, depends on representative field data and an external velocity source for training labels, and assumes conventional fixed-radius wheel slip
Relevance to This Thesis: Shows that proprioceptive learning can classify slip without visual odometry, but does not provide online per-spoke contact selection or directly correct an inertial state estimate
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections 3–5; Tables 1–2; Figures 4–6
Notes: The paper calls the algorithms unsupervised, but explicitly states that a small amount of labelled data is required to associate learned clusters with semantic slip classes.

Paper Title: Visual-Inertial-Wheel Odometry with Slip Compensation and Dynamic Feature Elimination
Method Category: Learning-based wheel-slip compensation within visual-inertial-wheel odometry
Robot Platform: Public KAIST urban robotic-platform dataset and a Clearpath Jackal four-wheel skid-steer UGV for indoor experiments
Sensors: Camera; six-axis IMU; wheel encoders; the slip-compensation model also uses encoder velocities and pre-integrated inertial yaw information
State Estimator: Multi-State Constraint Kalman Filter (MSCKF)
Estimated State Variables: Current inertial orientation, position, velocity, gyroscope bias, and accelerometer bias; cloned historical poses; wheel-odometry pose state
Propagation Model: Nonlinear IMU kinematics propagate the MSCKF state
Measurement or Update Model: Visual feature constraints are filtered by a feature-confidence estimator; wheel-odometry increments are corrected by sparse Gaussian-process regression using CNN- or LSTM-based deep kernels before updating the MSCKF
Slip Handling Type: Continuous learned odometry-error estimation and wheel-slip compensation; dynamic visual-feature elimination is handled separately
Contact Assumption: Planar differential-drive kinematics with conventional left and right wheel velocities and continuous wheel-ground interaction
Training or Prior Data Required: Supervised residual learning from physical wheel odometry and ground-truth pose increments; Adam optimizer, 100 epochs, 100 Hz samples; inducing points are used for sparse GPR
Ground Truth Used: Ground-truth trajectories supplied with the KAIST dataset for outdoor evaluation; OptiTrack motion capture for indoor Jackal experiments
Validation Type: Evaluation on long real-world urban datasets plus controlled real-robot indoor experiments
Terrain or Environment: Dynamic urban environments in the KAIST dataset; indoor floor experiments including gravel and sand layers
Main Finding: Correcting wheel-odometry increments with a recurrent deep-kernel GPR and removing dynamic visual features improves VIWO trajectory and rotation accuracy over uncorrected VIWO
Quantitative Result: On the KAIST evaluation, MATE decreases from 11.45 m for uncorrected VIWO to 5.45 m with dynamic-point elimination and RNN-GPR slip compensation; MRTE decreases from 2.526 degrees to 1.079 degrees
Author-Stated Limitation: The authors identify future needs for more advanced temporal models, unsupervised or self-supervised adaptation, and terrain-aware modelling for changing friction, inclination, and deformability
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The approach requires cameras, ground-truth training data, and a comparatively complex learned model; it assumes continuous differential-drive kinematics and does not model individual rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Demonstrates data-driven correction of slip-affected wheel odometry rather than rejecting measurements, providing a useful contrast to the proposed lightweight proprioceptive PCV gating approach
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections 3.1–3.2, 4–7; Tables 1–4
Notes: The paper evaluates slip compensation together with dynamic visual-feature elimination, so the reported overall improvement should not be attributed solely to the slip-compensation component.

Paper Title: Learning Wheel Odometry and IMU Errors for Localization
Method Category: Gaussian-process learning of wheel-odometry and IMU model residuals
Robot Platform: Segway platform in the University of Michigan North Campus Long-Term Vision and LiDAR dataset; consumer car in the Complex Urban LiDAR dataset
Sensors: Two wheel encoders; one-axis fiber-optic gyroscope; low-cost three-axis gyroscopes and accelerometers in a commodity IMU
State Estimator: Error-state Extended Kalman Filter built from learned propagation and observation models
Estimated State Variables: Three-dimensional position, Euler-angle orientation, and body-frame angular rates
Propagation Model: Planar wheel-speed and fiber-optic-gyro kinematic model whose state increments are corrected using a multi-output Gaussian process with neural-network deep kernels
Measurement or Update Model: Integrated IMU pseudo-measurements update the EKF; both propagation and observation-model residuals can be learned from ground truth
Slip Handling Type: Learned generic odometry and inertial-model error correction; wheel slip is not explicitly detected, classified, or rejected
Contact Assumption: Conventional two-wheel planar-motion model with implicit continuous wheel contact
Training or Prior Data Required: Per-dataset supervised training from ground-truth poses and sensor logs; data split into 70% training, 15% cross-validation, and 15% testing; neural deep-kernel GP with stochastic variational inference
Ground Truth Used: Ground-truth poses provided by the two public datasets; ground-truth speed is obtained by differentiating position
Validation Type: Offline evaluation on two public real-world datasets
Terrain or Environment: Long-term campus Segway operation and urban consumer-car trajectories
Main Finding: Learning residual corrections improves both wheel-odometry dead reckoning and EKF localization using wheel encoders, fiber-optic gyro, and IMU data
Quantitative Result: For the corrected propagation model, translation m-ATE decreases from 0.160 m to 0.091 m on the Segway dataset and from 0.035 m to 0.024 m on the car dataset; rotation m-ATE decreases from 2.75 to 1.78 degrees and from 0.49 to 0.24 degrees, respectively; corrected EKFs also outperform physical-model EKFs, although exact filter values are shown graphically rather than tabulated
Author-Stated Limitation: Future work is required to determine what the Gaussian process learns, test generalization across datasets, and develop online learning using numerous but inaccurate ground-truth measurements
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The corrections are learned separately for each dataset from external ground truth, the method does not explicitly distinguish slip from other modelling errors, and it assumes a continuous conventional-wheel model
Relevance to This Thesis: Relevant as a learning-based odometry correction approach and as evidence that unmodelled wheel and IMU errors can be learned, but it is not itself a slip detector and should not be described as one
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections II–VI; Figure 2 and Figures 6–8
Notes: Use this paper under learning-based odometry-error correction rather than claiming that it explicitly estimates wheel slip.

Paper Title: Center of Mass Dynamics and Contact-Aided Invariant Filtering for Biped Robot State Estimation
Method Category: Contact-aided invariant filtering with center-of-mass dynamics
Robot Platform: Defensor hydraulic biped robot model (12 actuated leg DoF; 115 kg)
Sensors: IMU; joint-position encoders; foot force/torque sensors
State Estimator: Right-invariant Extended Kalman Filter on an extended matrix Lie group
Estimated State Variables: Floating-base rotation, velocity, and position; center-of-mass velocity and position; contact-point position; gyroscope and accelerometer biases
Propagation Model: IMU-based floating-base propagation augmented with center-of-mass dynamics derived from measured contact forces
Measurement or Update Model: Contact-limb forward kinematics and center-of-mass forward kinematics provide right-invariant measurement updates
Slip Handling Type: Slip-robust auxiliary dynamics; no explicit slip detection or measurement rejection
Contact Assumption: A detected support limb supplies a contact-kinematic constraint, while contact-point motion is represented with slippage noise; CoM dynamics are treated as less sensitive to contact slip
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; requires robot kinematics, mass/dynamic model, and force-sensor measurements
Ground Truth Used: Ground-truth states from the Simscape Multibody simulation
Terrain or Environment: Simulated biped walking with induced foot slip at several commanded speeds
Main Finding: Adding center-of-mass dynamics reduces floating-base velocity error in the direction affected by contact slip and directly provides center-of-mass state estimates
Quantitative Result: The floating-base velocity RMSE in the principal slip direction was reduced by 23.6% in the reported comparison; improvements were also reported at multiple reference speeds
Author-Stated Limitation: The authors note that contact information should be trusted more under external-force disturbances, whereas CoM information should be trusted more during contact slip; automatically determining this credibility is left for future work
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method requires foot force measurements and a biped dynamic model, and it does not select among multiple geometric potential contacts such as rimless-wheel spokes
Relevance to This Thesis: Shows how contact-aided InEKF performance degrades under slipping contacts and how an additional proprioceptive dynamic constraint can improve robustness
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections III-V; Section VII and Tables I-II; Sections VIII-IX
Notes: Useful as contact-aided InEKF literature, but the proposed CoM-force update is not directly transferable to the Coyote 3 sensor configuration

Paper Title: Estimation of Human Base Kinematics using Dynamical Inverse Kinematics and Contact-Aided Lie Group Kalman Filter
Method Category: Contact-aided Lie-group filtering for floating-base kinematics
Robot Platform: Human subject represented by a floating-base articulated rigid-body model
Sensors: Distributed wearable IMUs; six-axis force/torque sensors in instrumented shoes; Vive tracker used only for evaluation
State Estimator: Extended Kalman Filter on a composite matrix Lie group, cascaded after dynamical inverse kinematics
Estimated State Variables: Floating-base pose and velocity; candidate foot-contact positions; foot orientations; joint states are estimated by the preceding dynamical inverse-kinematics block
Propagation Model: Inertial floating-base propagation using pelvis-IMU angular velocity together with contact-state dynamics
Measurement or Update Model: Center-of-pressure-based contact detection; relative contact-point position, contact-derived base velocity, foot orientation, and pelvis-IMU measurements are fused as invariant and non-invariant observations
Slip Handling Type: No explicit slip detection or compensation; contacts are inferred from force distribution and assumed usable while active
Contact Assumption: Candidate points on each foot are treated as fixed contacts while the foot remains attached to the ground
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; requires calibrated human kinematic model and known IMU-to-link transformations
Ground Truth Used: Vive motion-tracking trajectory mounted on the base/pelvis
Terrain or Environment: Flat-surface standing, walking, and turning with a sensorized suit and shoes
Main Finding: The cascaded dynamical-IK and contact-aided Lie-group filter reconstructs whole-body motion without external position measurements during estimation and produces base trajectories broadly comparable with the Vive reference
Quantitative Result: No numerical pose-error metric is reported; the evaluation is qualitative through trajectory and motion-reconstruction plots
Author-Stated Limitation: The authors report peaks in the estimates during contact events and note limited accuracy in the alignment between the estimated and Vive trajectories
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The contact model is designed for flat human feet with force-sensing shoes and does not address rolling, discontinuous, or uncertain rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Provides an example of contact detection feeding a Lie-group base estimator, but it is peripheral because the platform and contact geometry differ substantially from a rimless-wheeled rover
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections III-VI; Section VII and Figures 2-4; Section VIII
Notes: Probably a supporting rather than core citation for the thesis SOTA

Paper Title: Proprioceptive-Based Multimode State Estimation for Wheeled-Quadruped Robot
Method Category: Proprioceptive multimode state estimation for wheeled-legged robots
Robot Platform: Lite3-W wheeled-quadruped robot with one driven wheel at the end of each leg
Sensors: IMU; motor encoders; joint torque measurements; motion capture, RTK, and a force plate used for evaluation
State Estimator: Standard Kalman Filter with probabilistic slip, moving-contact, contact-detection, and vertical-contact-constraint modules
Estimated State Variables: Body position and velocity together with per-leg wheel-contact position/velocity-related states in a 24-state formulation
Propagation Model: IMU acceleration and body motion are used for prediction, with wheel/leg kinematics contributing to the motion model
Measurement or Update Model: A probabilistic slip model estimates wheel-induced displacement; an offset wheel-leg contact model estimates moving contact position and velocity; vertical contact constraints and normalized momentum-based contact detection are included in the update
Slip Handling Type: Model-based slip compensation and contact-aware measurement construction; no complete rejection of wheel measurements
Contact Assumption: Each tire has a single instantaneous contact point that moves along the tire tread; contact state is estimated probabilistically
Training or Prior Data Required: No learned training data; requires robot geometry and manually selected probability-model parameters
Ground Truth Used: NOKOV motion capture indoors; BeiDou RTK outdoors; force plate for contact-state validation
Terrain or Environment: Indoor flat-floor wheeled, legged, and hybrid motion; outdoor multimode trajectories longer than 100 m
Main Finding: Explicitly modelling wheel-induced displacement, moving wheel-ground contacts, and contact constraints improves horizontal and vertical state estimation across wheeled, legged, and hybrid modes
Quantitative Result: The method achieved at least a 1.75-times RMSE reduction across all directions versus existing methods; contact-model ablations showed at least 30% lower RMSE and 56% lower end-to-end error
Author-Stated Limitation: Outdoor RTK vertical position was not accurate enough to serve as height ground truth; future work targets terrain estimation for more complex environments
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method still assumes a continuous tire surface with one moving contact point and relies on a platform-specific probabilistic slip model; it does not model discrete spoke contacts or innovation-based rejection
Relevance to This Thesis: Highly relevant comparison for adapting contact-aided estimation to a hybrid wheeled-legged platform where the conventional fixed-foot assumption is invalid
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections II-A-II-D; Section III and Tables I-III; Section IV
Notes: Strong core paper for the wheel-legged/contact-aided subsection

Paper Title: A Multi-Modal Fusion Framework for State Estimation in Four-Wheel-Legged Robots
Method Category: Probabilistic contact estimation and wheel-IMU state estimation
Robot Platform: Simulated four-wheel-legged robot
Sensors: Gait-phase information; knee flexion/extension joint torque; wheel-bottom height; motor encoders; IMU
State Estimator: Two Kalman-filter modules: multi-probability contact estimator and position/velocity estimator
Estimated State Variables: Wheel-ground contact probabilities; robot center position and velocity in the world frame
Propagation Model: Kalman prediction using gait/contact probability for the contact estimator and motion/IMU information for body-state prediction
Measurement or Update Model: Contact probability from gait phase, knee-joint torque, and wheel-bottom height is fused; encoder kinematics and IMU attitude/angular-rate information update position and velocity
Slip Handling Type: No explicit slip estimation or rejection; contact-state fusion is intended to improve robustness during mode switching and uncertain interaction
Contact Assumption: Wheel-bottom contact is represented as a binary state obtained by thresholding a fused probability at 0.4
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; probability distributions and thresholds are manually defined from the robot model and simulation behavior
Ground Truth Used: Webots simulation truth
Terrain or Environment: Webots pure rolling and mixed wheel-walking motion
Main Finding: Fusing three contact-probability cues provides contact estimates consistent with simulation truth, while encoder-IMU fusion tracks body position and velocity
Quantitative Result: Reported contact-state discrimination accuracy remained above 90%; the authors claim a 30-40% hardware-cost reduction, while no numerical position/velocity RMSE is provided
Author-Stated Limitation: Future work is required for unstructured terrain, visual-sensor integration, and computational optimization for real-time deployment
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method is validated only in simulation, uses a manually thresholded binary wheel-bottom contact state, and does not model individual potential contacts or slip residuals
Relevance to This Thesis: Relevant mainly as a wheel-legged contact-estimation example, but weaker evidence than real-robot studies and not based on invariant filtering
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections II-III; Section IV and Figures 7-11; Section V
Notes: Use cautiously because the pose-estimation results are qualitative and the work is simulation-only

Paper Title: Adaptive Invariant Extended Kalman Filter for Legged Robot State Estimation
Method Category: Adaptive contact-aided invariant filtering with slip handling
Robot Platform: LeoQuad quadruped robot
Sensors: IMU; joint encoders/kinematics; commanded joint torques for contact detection; motion capture for evaluation; no dedicated contact sensors
State Estimator: Right-Invariant Extended Kalman Filter with online foot-noise covariance estimation and Mahalanobis-distance slip rejection
Estimated State Variables: Body rotation, velocity, and position; positions of the contacting feet; accelerometer and gyroscope biases
Propagation Model: Bias-corrected IMU propagation with static-foot process models whose noise is varied by contact state
Measurement or Update Model: Foot position kinematics provide right-invariant measurements; contact is estimated from a generalized-momentum method; per-axis foot-model noise is adapted online and large slips can be rejected using Mahalanobis distance
Slip Handling Type: Adaptive measurement/process-noise scaling plus threshold-based slip rejection
Contact Assumption: Contacting feet are nominally stationary, but deviations are represented through adaptively estimated foot-velocity noise
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; requires window-size, covariance, contact-detection, and slip-threshold tuning
Ground Truth Used: Motion-capture system
Terrain or Environment: Flat and rough terrain with trot, flying-trot, and pronk gaits; 60-second trials
Main Finding: Adaptive foot-noise estimation handles small or axis-specific slips that binary rejection can miss and improves robustness during highly dynamic locomotion
Quantitative Result: For rough-terrain flying trot, velocity RMSE changed from 0.110/0.056/0.145 m/s with conventional IEKF to 0.048/0.050/0.022 m/s with combined slip rejection and foot-noise estimation; pitch RMSE decreased from 0.292 to 0.190 degrees, while roll RMSE changed from 0.337 to 0.356 degrees
Author-Stated Limitation: Future work includes refining noise-parameter tuning, extending the method to other robots, and improving unobservable position and yaw estimation
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method assumes leg-foot contacts and augments foot positions in the state; it does not address the geometry-driven choice among multiple rimless-wheel spokes or measured spoke-tip velocity
Relevance to This Thesis: Very relevant because it compares fixed-noise contact updates, Mahalanobis slip rejection, and adaptive covariance handling within an InEKF
Evidence Page or Section: Abstract; Sections III-A-III-D; Section IV and Table I; Section V
Notes: This is an arXiv preprint dated October 2025; verify the final publication status before citing it as peer-reviewed

Paper Title: State Estimation for Wheeled-Legged Robot with Slip Detection
Method Category: Slip-aware wheeled-legged state estimation using slip-ratio-dependent covariance adaptation
Robot Platform: Custom single wheeled-legged robot constrained to one-dimensional longitudinal motion
Sensors: IMU; leg-joint and wheel encoders; wheel-motor current/torque; contact or ground-pressure information; RGB camera with AprilTag used only for ground truth
State Estimator: Linear 15-state Kalman Filter
Estimated State Variables: Torso position and velocity; wheel-motion-contributed torso position and velocity; contact-point position
Propagation Model: IMU- and kinematics-based propagation of torso and contact-related states
Measurement or Update Model: Contact-point position, velocity from leg extension, and velocity from wheel rotation update the filter; measurement covariance is scaled using contact confidence and estimated slip ratio
Slip Handling Type: Slip-ratio estimation and adaptive measurement covariance; wheel information is given negligible influence when the estimated slip ratio exceeds approximately 0.2
Contact Assumption: Contact is represented by a contact-phase/height confidence term; the effective wheel radius is derived from the leg geometry; the current formulation assumes flat horizontal terrain
Training or Prior Data Required: No learned training data; requires robot dynamics, motor torque from current, ground-pressure/contact information, and hand-tuned observer and covariance gains
Ground Truth Used: Gazebo true torso state in simulation; AprilTag position from a fixed 75 Hz RGB camera and differentiated velocity in hardware experiments
Terrain or Environment: Low- and high-friction Gazebo surfaces; soapy POM plastic board, foam floor mat, and stepping with intermittent wheel contact
Main Finding: Estimating slip ratio and reducing confidence in wheel-derived measurements improves longitudinal position and velocity estimation during acceleration, braking, re-adhesion, and stepping
Quantitative Result: In the hardware acceleration/deceleration test, average longitudinal position relative error decreased from 399.26% to 10.59%, and average velocity relative error decreased from 231.15% to 22.86%; improvements were reported in all three scenarios
Author-Stated Limitation: The slip-ratio estimator requires ground-pressure input and lacks terrain-slope identification, so it is currently limited to flat horizontal terrain; during severe slip or when the wheel is airborne, the filter relies mainly on the IMU
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The experiments use a single wheel-leg constrained to one axis and do not address selection among multiple discontinuous rimless-wheel spokes; the method also depends on contact force and effective-radius modeling
Relevance to This Thesis: Highly relevant as an example of slip-aware covariance adaptation for a wheel-legged robot; it contrasts with the proposed approach, which uses contact-velocity innovation consistency and can reject individual spoke measurements
Evidence Page or Section: Sections III-A and III-B; Section IV; Section V; Tables I-IV; Section VI
Notes: The slip ratio is used to alter measurement confidence rather than directly correcting the measured wheel velocity

Paper Title: A Dynamic-Model-Based Wheel Slip Detector for Mobile Robots on Outdoor Terrain
Method Category: Dynamic-model-based longitudinal slip and immobilization detection
Robot Platform: DARPA LAGR four-wheel outdoor mobile robot with front-wheel differential drive and pneumatic tires
Sensors: Front-wheel encoders; Xsens MT9 IMU; optional Garmin differential GPS
State Estimator: Extended Kalman Filter
Estimated State Variables: Longitudinal body velocity; accelerometer walking bias; longitudinal disturbance acceleration; left and right wheel angular velocities; yaw rate; yaw-gyro walking bias
Propagation Model: Nonlinear vehicle dynamics using a tire traction/braking model, gravity, rolling resistance, external disturbance force, and wheel-controller dynamics
Measurement or Update Model: Asynchronous updates from longitudinal IMU acceleration, IMU yaw rate, GPS longitudinal velocity, and left/right wheel-encoder angular velocities; weak physical constraints limit poorly observable states
Slip Handling Type: Longitudinal slip estimation and threshold-based immobilization detection; no wheel-measurement rejection or slip compensation
Contact Assumption: Conventional continuous tire-ground contact represented by a terrain-dependent traction/braking model
Training or Prior Data Required: Requires robot mass and geometry, IMU-noise parameters, and tire/rolling-resistance model parameters estimated from simple experiments and manually tuned
Ground Truth Used: Immobilization events were deliberately generated by restraining the robot with a spring scale; GPS supplied velocity when available, but independent ground-truth velocity was not used for full estimator validation
Validation Type: Real-robot outdoor experiments with offline MATLAB processing and an online C++ implementation
Terrain or Environment: Approximately 120 m over loose mulch, loose gravel on dry soil, mud, and several grass surfaces; speeds from 0.1 to 1.0 m/s
Main Finding: The dynamic model and proprioceptive measurements detect complete immobilization quickly and remain effective when GPS is unavailable
Quantitative Result: All 20 immobilization events in 21 runs were detected with an average detection time of 0.4 s; fewer than 0.2% of data points were falsely labelled with GPS, and 0.35% without GPS
Author-Stated Limitation: Velocity accuracy depends on the terrain-specific tire model; future work calls for independent velocity ground truth, online tire-parameter adaptation, a lateral-force model for sideslip, and fusion of multiple detectors
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method models aggregate vehicle-level tire slip and complete immobilization under continuous wheel contact; it does not handle individual, intermittent rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Provides a strong model-based proprioceptive slip-detection baseline and demonstrates that slip can be detected without GPS, but its terrain and tire model differs fundamentally from PCV-based per-contact consistency testing
Evidence Page or Section: Sections II-III; Section IV-A to IV-E; Tables I-III; Section V
Notes: The algorithm estimates degree of longitudinal slip, but the demonstrated decision output focuses on whole-robot immobilization

Paper Title: Sequential track fusion in multi-sensor networks of unscented Kalman filters: A case of slip estimation in planetary mobile robots
Method Category: Proprioceptive slip estimation using track-to-track UKF fusion
Robot Platform: Simulated six-wheel lunar wheeled mobile robot with steerable wheels
Sensors: IMU; steering-angle measurements; wheel encoders
State Estimator: Network of local Unscented Kalman Filters fused with sequential SLR/UKF track-to-track fusion
Estimated State Variables: Body longitudinal velocity; body lateral velocity; yaw rate; per-wheel-set longitudinal slip ratio derived from estimated motion and encoder speed
Propagation Model: Continuous-time rigid-body velocity model driven by IMU linear and angular acceleration, discretized with Euler-Maruyama
Measurement or Update Model: Each steerable wheel-set agent uses its steering angle as a measurement; local UKF tracks are fused sequentially while propagating cross-correlations
Slip Handling Type: Online longitudinal slip-ratio estimation; no measurement rejection
Contact Assumption: Ackermann steering geometry; negligible lateral slip angle; fixed-radius conventional wheels with continuous contact
Training or Prior Data Required: No training; model and covariance parameters are specified for simulation
Ground Truth Used: True states from a high-fidelity software-in-the-loop digital-twin simulation
Validation Type: High-fidelity software-in-the-loop simulation with 200 Monte Carlo runs
Terrain or Environment: Simulated six-wheel lunar rover using a Vortex Studio wheel-soil contact model; high- and low-traction straight motion and circular motion
Main Finding: Sequential SLR/UKF track fusion provides consistent real-time slip estimates while balancing accuracy and computational cost better than the compared consistent fusion methods
Quantitative Result: Reported ANEES values were 1.1003, 1.0662, and 0.9288 for high-traction straight, low-traction straight, and circular tests; corresponding ARMSE values were 0.1983, 0.0012, and 0.0004
Author-Stated Limitation: Integration of the estimator into the rover feedback-control system is left as immediate future work; no real-world experiment is presented
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method assumes negligible lateral slip and continuous fixed-radius wheel contact and estimates aggregate wheel-set slip rather than the consistency of individual rimless-wheel spoke contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Relevant as a purely proprioceptive planetary-rover slip estimator, but its purpose is continuous slip-ratio estimation rather than per-contact measurement rejection
Evidence Page or Section: Sections IV-V; Equations 38-43; Table I; Section VI
Notes: This paper was already included in the wheel-IMU fusion batch; do not duplicate it in the master spreadsheet

Paper Title: Modeling of slip rate-dependent traversability for path planning of wheeled mobile robot in sandy terrain
Method Category: Terramechanics-inspired slip prediction and slip-aware path planning
Robot Platform: Instrumented single-wheel testbed and simulated planetary rover
Sensors: Linear encoder for carriage velocity; wheel-motor encoder; vertical displacement measurement; six-axis force/torque sensor
State Estimator: No recursive state estimator; slip is predicted with an experimentally fitted regression model
Estimated State Variables: Slip ratio predicted from traction force and slip rate for path-planning cost evaluation
Propagation Model: Not applicable to state estimation; rover dynamics and terrain slope are used in planning simulations
Measurement or Update Model: A slip-rate-dependent regression model maps traction force and slip rate to predicted slip ratio, which is inserted into the path-planning cost function
Slip Handling Type: Offline model-based slip prediction and slip-minimizing path planning; no online slip detection, compensation, or measurement rejection
Contact Assumption: Continuous interaction between a lugged circular wheel and deformable Toyoura sand; the model is wheel-, load-, speed-, and terrain-dependent
Training or Prior Data Required: Requires single-wheel experiments over a range of imposed slip rates, wheel speeds, and wheel designs to fit the regression model
Ground Truth Used: Directly commanded/measured slip ratio, traction force, wheel speed, carriage speed, and sinkage on the testbed; simulated rover states in planning studies
Validation Type: Physical single-wheel experiments plus path-planning and dynamics simulations
Terrain or Environment: Toyoura sand in a single-wheel testbed; simulated crater and rough-terrain traversal
Main Finding: Slip ratio at a given traction force changes with slip rate, and including this transient effect in the planning cost can select paths with less predicted slip
Quantitative Result: The proposed regression achieved an average adjusted R-squared of 0.847; planning simulations reported a 66% slip-ratio reduction near a crater and 58% on rough terrain
Author-Stated Limitation: Experiments used a small, lightly loaded, slowly rotating wheel on flat terrain; more data, slope experiments, rough-terrain validation, improved transient simulation, and online replanning are needed
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method is an offline terrain- and wheel-specific planning model rather than an odometry or state-estimation method, and its continuous-wheel assumptions do not represent intermittent rimless-wheel contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Useful as supporting literature for model-based slip prediction and the terrain dependence of wheel slip, but it should not be presented as a direct alternative to the proposed CVO estimator
Evidence Page or Section: Sections 3-5; Figures 1-9; Tables 3-5; Conclusion and Future Work
Notes: The Zotero metadata incorrectly labels the attachment as 2026; the article was published on 25 January 2024

Paper Title: Invariant Smoother for Legged Robot State Estimation With Dynamic Contact Event Information
Method Category: Invariant fixed-lag smoothing with dynamic-contact slip rejection
Robot Platform: KAIST HOUND quadruped robot
Sensors: IMU and joint encoders; Vicon motion capture used for indoor ground truth
State Estimator: Right-invariant fixed-lag smoother on a matrix Lie group
Estimated State Variables: Base rotation, velocity, and position; positions of contacting feet; gyroscope bias; accelerometer bias
Propagation Model: IMU integration for base motion with zero-velocity propagation of feet assumed to be in stable contact
Measurement or Update Model: Leg forward-kinematic observations and a contact-loop constraint connect foot positions across multiple timesteps during stable contact
Slip Handling Type: Estimated-foot-velocity thresholding with covariance inflation; slip status and past foot velocities are re-evaluated within the smoothing window rather than hard-rejecting measurements
Contact Assumption: Feet are assumed static during contact; when estimated foot velocity exceeds a threshold, the static-contact constraint is assigned a larger slip covariance
Training or Prior Data Required: No learned model; requires robot kinematics and empirically selected contact/slip covariances, velocity and acceleration thresholds, and smoother window size
Ground Truth Used: Nine-camera Vicon system at 200 Hz for five indoor 120 s experiments; no quantitative ground truth was available for the 160 m outdoor test
Validation Type: Online real-robot experiments and offline comparisons with InEKF and noninvariant smoothers
Terrain or Environment: Slippery floor, deformable mattress, stacked wooden plates with pebbles, external pulling disturbance, and a 160 m outdoor figure-eight trajectory
Main Finding: State-independent invariant Jacobians improve optimization convergence, while smoothing enables re-evaluation of dynamic contact events and improves robustness when the static-foot assumption is violated
Quantitative Result: During slip, the pseudo-invariant smoother required up to approximately 25 times more iterations than the proposed invariant smoother; the proposed estimator ran online at 200 Hz and completed a 160 m outdoor trajectory
Author-Stated Limitation: Estimator frequency was limited to 200 Hz by onboard computation; future work includes exteroceptive measurements, loop closure, nonlinear constraints, more precise terrain/contact uncertainty models, and a more rigorous discrete-time derivation
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The method is designed for articulated feet with joint-kinematic contact constraints and does not model wheel-encoder-driven spoke-tip velocity or select among multiple potential rimless-wheel contacts
Relevance to This Thesis: Highly relevant for explaining covariance inflation and re-evaluation of contact validity under slip; it offers a smoother-based alternative to the proposed filter-based PCV gating
Evidence Page or Section: Sections IV-V; Section VII; Figures 9-15; Tables III-IV; Section VIII
Notes: The Zotero export contains the same PDF twice; include only one literature-matrix row

Paper Title: Model-Based Slippage Estimation to Enhance Planetary Rover Localization with Wheel Odometry
Method Category: Terramechanics-based slip estimation and wheel-odometry correction
Robot Platform: Simulated six-wheel, three-axle rover based on the ESA ExoMars rover with independently steered wheels
Sensors: Wheel encoders providing wheel angular rates; IMU providing heading and yaw-rate measurements
State Estimator: Model-based dead-reckoning estimator; no Kalman filter
Estimated State Variables: Planar position, heading, yaw rate, longitudinal velocity, and lateral velocity
Propagation Model: Nonlinear rover dynamics with compliant-wheel/compliant-soil interaction forces and Ackermann steering
Measurement or Update Model: Wheel angular-rate and IMU heading/yaw-rate measurements are periodically integrated through the dynamic model to predict slip and propagate the rover state
Slip Handling Type: Model-based longitudinal and lateral slip estimation followed by correction of wheel-odometry measurements
Contact Assumption: Continuous compliant circular-wheel contact with homogeneous terrain properties; wheel-soil interaction is described by a terramechanics model
Training or Prior Data Required: No learned training data; requires rover geometry and a rough prior characterization of soil parameters and terrain slope
Ground Truth Used: Numerically simulated rover trajectory and states
Terrain or Environment: Martian-like cloddy, mixed drift-cloddy, and drift soils with slopes and randomly distributed hazards
Main Finding: Predicting wheel slip with a detailed rover-terrain dynamic model substantially improves dead-reckoning localization compared with uncorrected wheel-odometry and IMU integration
Quantitative Result: The authors report that the final position error is approximately three times lower than with uncorrected wheel odometry; the plotted final error decreases from about 60-70 m to about 20 m over roughly 360 m of travel
Author-Stated Limitation: The simulations assume homogeneous and constant soil characteristics along the path and require a rough prior description of the environment; validation is limited to simulation
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The terrain- and wheel-specific continuous-contact model is difficult to transfer to a rimless wheel with intermittent individual spoke contacts and would require terrain parameters that may be unavailable online
Relevance to This Thesis: Useful as a model-based slip-compensation alternative to the proposed innovation-based contact-velocity rejection; it belongs mainly in the model-based slip section rather than the rimless-wheel odometry section
Evidence Page or Section: Sections 2.1-2.2; Section 4.2; Figures 13-16; Section 5
Notes: The estimator samples wheel-encoder and IMU data at 60 s intervals in the numerical localization experiment

Paper Title: Gait Analysis and Control of Rimless Wheel Rovers for Planetary Exploration
Method Category: Rimless-wheel gait classification, gait control, and body-motion analysis
Robot Platform: Coyote II four-wheel rimless-wheeled rover; evaluation uses a simplified Drake simulation model with four five-spoke wheels
Sensors: The simulated controller uses wheel joint states and contact events; the physical Coyote II platform includes an IMU, LiDAR, and stereo cameras, but these are not used for state estimation in the study
State Estimator: None
Estimated State Variables: No odometry state is estimated; the study evaluates body vertical displacement and roll, pitch, and yaw motion
Propagation Model: Drake rigid-body simulation with sinusoidal wheel-leg actuation and prescribed inter-wheel phase offsets
Measurement or Update Model: A gait controller adjusts wheel angular-velocity commands using contact events and phase shifts inferred from joint angular positions
Slip Handling Type: None
Contact Assumption: The wheels complete cycles with equal period, have no deliberate flight phase, and the rover moves forward on a level, rigid, unobstructed surface
Training or Prior Data Required: No training data; predefined gait phase offsets and a simplified robot model are used
Ground Truth Used: Simulation states from Drake
Terrain or Environment: Level rigid surface in an unobstructed simulated environment
Main Finding: The relative phase of the four rimless wheels strongly affects vertical and rotational motion of the rover body, showing that spoke-contact sequencing influences platform vibration and potentially sensor quality
Quantitative Result: Pace and Trot produced the smallest vertical peak-to-peak motion at 1.7 mm, followed by Walk at 1.8 mm; Pronk and Bound produced 28.2 mm. Table I also reports gait-dependent roll, pitch, and yaw amplitudes
Author-Stated Limitation: The analysis uses a simplified simulation model; some yaw drift is attributed to model simplification, and hardware validation plus implementation of a suitable contact detector are left for future work
Limitation Relevant to This Thesis: The work does not estimate odometry, slip, or contact velocity and is restricted to regular gait motion on a level rigid surface
Relevance to This Thesis: Strong platform-specific background showing that discontinuous spoke contacts and wheel phase relationships create gait-dependent body motion; it should support the motivation for rimless-wheel-specific odometry rather than be presented as an odometry method
Evidence Page or Section: Sections I-IV; Sections V-VII; Table I; Figures 7-9
Notes: The paper cites two more directly relevant rimless-wheel localization works: Hidalgo-Carrio et al. (2014) on improved odometry and Schwendner et al. (2014) on embodied-data localization and mapping
Final Section: Odometry for Rimless-Wheeled and Hybrid Wheel--Leg Robots
